title: Google ships Gemini 3.6 Flash and 3.5 Flash-Lite for agentic workloads
subtitle: The Flash tier gets cheaper, more token-efficient models tuned for tool-using agents while the delayed 3.5 Pro remains missing.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Google released Gemini 3.6 Flash and Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite on July 21, positioning both as the working horses of the agentic era rather than the flagship. Flash 3.6 lists at $1.50 per million input tokens and $7.50 per million output tokens — down from the $9 output price of 3.5 Flash — with an advertised 17 percent reduction in output token usage and a knowledge cutoff pushed forward to March 2026. Flash-Lite comes in at $0.30 input and $2.50 output, aimed at high-volume, low-latency tasks. Notably absent: Gemini 3.5 Pro, which has missed multiple release targets and is still limited to an enterprise preview.

The Flash tier matters more than the flagship right now because agentic workflows chew through tokens at a rate that punishes premium pricing. A model that is fast enough to call in a loop, cheap enough to leave running, and good enough to hand real work to is what the tooling economy actually needs — and where Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic are all fighting hardest. Google is claiming both a price cut and a token-efficiency gain in the same release, which is the combination developers care about.

The launch also reframes the delayed 3.5 Pro problem. Google has previewed Gemini 4 pretraining runs while conceding that Pro is not ready, a sequence that would have been unusual a year ago and now reads as normal for the frontier: incremental Flash-tier improvements ship on schedule, top-of-line models slip. The context is that the last six months have seen every major lab miss at least one flagship deadline while continuing to lower per-token costs on the mid-tier models developers actually use in production.

For learners, the through-line here is: the model that runs in your agent loop is more important than the one on the benchmark chart. Read the pricing page before the leaderboard. Cost per successful task, not cost per token, is the metric that will decide which model your future employer is running — and Flash-tier releases like this one are where that math changes.
